what_it_does: A headless Stage 0 acceptance test that runs a two-node constant-to-uppercase graph through the real filesystem store and scheduler, checking reactive firing, correct output, memoized re-runs, change propagation, and resume after a fresh-process restart.
observations: Integration/acceptance test, not a unit test
Uses real FsStore and real scheduler, not mocks or AI
Builds a Graph with one constant node and one uppercase node connected by a single edge
Runs scheduler four times to cover first execution, unchanged re-run, changed input, and fresh-process resume
Checks both which nodes ran/skipped and the final uppercase output
Uses a small _check helper that prints PASS/FAIL and accumulates an overall ok flag
Returns 0 on success and 1 on failure
standouts: It is explicitly framed as the 'walking skeleton' end-to-end spine test
It verifies memoization semantics by asserting the second run executes nothing
It verifies process restart resume by creating a brand-new FsStore object over the same directory
conventions: Top-level module docstring explains stage and run command
sys.path.insert plus noqa:E402 for local imports
Manual pass/fail helper instead of pytest/unittest
Returns an exit code from main
concerns: Hardcoded /tmp/company-skeleton-store can collide across parallel runs or leave state behind
No cleanup of the temporary store after the test finishes
No use of a standard test framework, so failure details are only printed
notes: This is the project's Stage 0 spine test: the simplest possible end-to-end proof that the scheduler, store, and two nodes work, memoize, propagate changes, and survive a process restart.
questions: What exact signature and return shape does scheduler.run provide?
How does the store compute and compare node signatures for memoization?
What does store.head return when no output exists?
Why does the uppercase node require no config?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the docstring says it proves reactive firing, correct result, memoization, change propagation, and resume after restart, and main performs exactly those checks.
intention_signals: Establish the smallest end-to-end confidence check for the runtime before layering on more complex features, ensuring the scheduler and store behave correctly with real node implementations.
novelty: The test explicitly treats a fresh-process restart as a first-class scenario by instantiating a new FsStore object over the same directory and asserting no work re-runs.
contribution: Provides an executable Stage 0 acceptance test that validates core scheduler/store semantics—reactive execution, memoization, incremental change propagation, and restart resume—using real node implementations.
summary_for_embedding: A headless acceptance test that exercises a constant-to-uppercase graph through the real filesystem store and scheduler to verify reactive execution, memoization, change propagation, and restart resume.
intention_for_embedding: To serve as the walking skeleton Stage 0 test proving the core reactive runtime works end-to-end before further features are added.

symbols.does: Constructs a two-node Graph with a constant source and an uppercase consumer connected by an edge.
symbols.description: build_graph(value) returns a Graph named 'demo' containing a constant node 'src' configured with the given value, an uppercase node 'up', and an edge from src.value to up.text.

symbols.does: Retrieves the stored output content for a given node under the demo run namespace.
symbols.description: output_of(store, node_id) looks up the current CAS address at run://demo/{node_id} via store.head and returns the decoded content, or None if no address is present.

symbols.does: Orchestrates the walking skeleton acceptance scenarios and prints the overall result.
symbols.description: main() wipes the store directory, builds the graph, and runs scheduler.run four times to check first-run behavior, memoized re-run, change propagation with a new value, and resume after a fresh-process restart, accumulating results through _check.

symbols.does: Prints a PASS/FAIL line for a labeled condition and returns the boolean.
symbols.description: _check(label, cond) is a minimal assertion helper used by main to report each scenario's result and accumulate the final ok flag without stopping at the first failure.
